title: SEGMENT 10 — Two-channel vs four-channel chemistry: the recent compression trick

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Two-channel vs four-channel chemistry: the recent compression trick
Alright, welcome back.

I want to start the second half with a piece of clever engineering that you might not encounter unless you read the Illumina chemistry notes carefully: the shift from four-channel chemistry to two-channel chemistry that happened in the mid-2010s on some of their instruments.
It's a small detail in some ways, but it shows how the platform has continued to be refined long after the original Solexa invention, and it illustrates how Illumina has driven cost down.

In the original Illumina chemistry — and still in some current instruments, including the MiSeq, the original HiSeq, and the latest NovaSeq X — each of the four bases is labeled with a distinct fluorescent dye.
A gets one color, C another, G another, T another.

To determine which base was just incorporated at each cluster, the instrument takes four separate exposures, one for each dye's emission wavelength, and looks at which color is brightest at each cluster position.
This is four-channel chemistry.
It's straightforward, accurate, and works fine.
But it has costs.
Four exposures means four times as much imaging time per cycle.
It means four lasers or filter changes per cycle, which adds mechanical complexity.
And it means the optical system has to be sensitive enough to distinguish four different wavelengths cleanly.
In 2014, with the launch of the NextSeq 500, Illumina introduced a clever alternative called two-channel chemistry, designed to halve the imaging time and simplify the optics.
The idea: use only two dyes — say, green and red — and encode the four bases as combinations of which dyes are present at each cluster.
Specifically: T is labeled with green dye only.
C is labeled with red dye only.
A is labeled with both green and red dyes — so it appears orange when imaged.
And G is labeled with no dye at all — so it appears dark.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine you're trying to distinguish four soccer team flags from a distance.
Four-channel chemistry is like having a flag of a different solid color for each team — red, blue, green, yellow.
You need a camera that can distinguish four colors.

Two-channel chemistry is like only having two flag colors — say, red and green — and using combinations to encode four teams: green flag only for one team, red flag only for another, both red and green flag for a third, no flag at all for a fourth.
You only need a camera that can see red and green.
You take two exposures instead of four.
And from the two-exposure data, you can still uniquely identify which team is at each cluster.
So in two-channel chemistry: green only means T, red only means C, both colors means A, no signal means G.
The instrument takes two exposures per cycle — one for green emission, one for red emission — extracts the per-cluster brightness in each, and looks up the combination to determine the base.
Faster, simpler, cheaper optics, with the same per-base accuracy as four-channel chemistry when implemented well.

There's a tradeoff: the "G is dark" base call relies on the absence of signal, which means that any unrelated source of dimness — a low-density cluster, a bubble in the flow cell, optical drift — can be mistakenly read as G.
Two-channel chemistry has a slight tendency to over-call G, particularly in clusters with low signal.
Illumina has tuned the chemistry and the base-calling algorithms over the years to manage this, and in practice the two-channel platforms perform very nearly as well as the four-channel ones.
The NovaSeq 6000 used a four-channel chemistry but with a different optimization to reduce imaging time.
The newest NovaSeq X uses yet another variant, called XLEAP-SBS, which streamlines the chemistry further with faster reactions and improved nucleotide design.
The general trend: each generation has chipped away at the per-cycle time and the per-base cost by improving the chemistry, the optics, or the base-calling algorithms, often in combination.
The platform from 2008 and the platform from 2024 are recognizably the same technology — same four-step cycle, same flow cell paradigm — but every component has been refined dozens of times over.
This is worth pointing out because it explains how Illumina maintained dominance for so long.
They didn't just hold the patents on the original Solexa chemistry.

They invested constantly in incremental improvement, year after year, layering optimization on optimization, and the cumulative result has been a roughly five hundred-fold reduction in cost per base since 2007.
The two-channel trick is one of many such optimizations.
The lesson, if you're thinking about how technology platforms maintain market leadership: relentless small improvement, compounded over a decade, can dwarf the impact of any single big invention.
The original chemistry was the seed.
The compounding optimization is what built the tree.
